coredata.dc:title: Route-dependent tailoring of carbon dot release in alginate hydrogel beads (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs): A versatile platform for biomedical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Nille, Omkar S.
Patil, Akshay S.
Vibhute, Anuja A.
Shendage, Shital S.
Tiwari, Arpita P.
Anbhule, Prashant V.
Sohn, Daewon
Gore, Anil H.
Kolekar, Govind B.
coredata.dc:description: The present investigation explores the different pathways for development of waste tea residue carbon dots (WTR-CDs) loading into hydrogel matrix for WTR-CDs releasing probe. Fluorescent WTR-CDs incorporated into hydrogel matrix were synthesized by valorisation of kitchen waste tea by simple carbonization method (λem = 450 nm, ΦWTR−CDs =18.45 %). Biopolymeric alginate-based hydrogel beads (HB-Alg) were prepared by simple extrusion method. Three routes (ex-situ/in-situ) were employed for loading of WTR-CDs into hydrogel matrix. Successful synthesis of WTR-CDs and its loading into hydrogel matrix was confirmed via various characterization techniques. Developed protocol was employed for stimuli-responsive cumulative release of WTR-CDs study (pH = 3.0, 7.4, 9.0) was monitored over 7 days. Results suggests that, the HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A system with in-situ loaded WTR-CDs have sustained release due to ionic interaction of WTR-CDs with crosslinked polymer network, whereas in HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B, WTR-CDs loaded in wet-beads having burst release in which loosely bound WTR-CDs into hydrogel cavities releases rapidly. While, in case of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C, lowest release was observed due to weakly surface bound WTR-CDs, low loading and shrinkage of pores into dry-beads. Radical scavenging activity was studied and shown antioxidant properties of WTR-Powder, WTR-CDs and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A,B,C. Cytotoxicity of all systems was checked via CAM assay and significant growth in blood vascularization with no loss of chick embryo confirming the released WTR-CDs are biocompatible. Successful investigation and summarization of results ensure that, waste-valorisation, simple, sustainable, and smart hydrogel systems with different routes of WTR-CDs loading have opened a window to understand the mechanistic pathways in release behaviour. This robust approach for improvement of smarter and biocompatible materials can be fruitfully applicable in advanced, controlled and stimuli responsive delivery probes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present investigation explores the different pathways for development of waste tea residue carbon dots (WTR-CDs) loading into hydrogel matrix for WTR-CDs releasing probe. Fluorescent WTR-CDs incorporated into hydrogel matrix were synthesized by valorisation of kitchen waste tea by simple carbonization method (λem = 450 nm, ΦWTR−CDs =18.45 %). Biopolymeric alginate-based hydrogel beads (HB-Alg) were prepared by simple extrusion method. Three routes (ex-situ/in-situ) were employed for loading of WTR-CDs into hydrogel matrix. Successful synthesis of WTR-CDs and its loading into hydrogel matrix was confirmed via various characterization techniques. Developed protocol was employed for stimuli-responsive cumulative release of WTR-CDs study (pH = 3.0, 7.4, 9.0) was monitored over 7 days. Results suggests that, the HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A system with in-situ loaded WTR-CDs have sustained release due to ionic interaction of WTR-CDs with crosslinked polymer network, whereas in HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B, WTR-CDs loaded in wet-beads having burst release in which loosely bound WTR-CDs into hydrogel cavities releases rapidly. While, in case of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C, lowest release was observed due to weakly surface bound WTR-CDs, low loading and shrinkage of pores into dry-beads. Radical scavenging activity was studied and shown antioxidant properties of WTR-Powder, WTR-CDs and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A,B,C. Cytotoxicity of all systems was checked via CAM assay and significant growth in blood vascularization with no loss of chick embryo confirming the released WTR-CDs are biocompatible. Successful investigation and summarization of results ensure that, waste-valorisation, simple, sustainable, and smart hydrogel systems with different routes of WTR-CDs loading have opened a window to understand the mechanistic pathways in release behaviour. This robust approach for improvement of smarter and biocompatible materials can be fruitfully applicable in advanced, controlled and stimuli responsive delivery probes.

ce:section.ce:para: The recent scientific evolution has been focused on developing a new class of environmentally friendly, sustainable as well as waste-to-wealth-based nanocomposites having simple, safer, and smarter multidisciplinary applicability. In last few decades, numerous health-related problems have aroused of which drug delivery probes are of great interest to the scientific society. The biopolymeric (sodium alginate, gelatin, chitosan, starch, etc.), physically or chemically crosslinked and three-dimensionally (3D) networked hydrogels have been highly recognized in pharmaceutical and biomedical applications [1]. Interestingly, biopolymeric hydrogels possess good mechanical strength, hydrophilicity, swellability, water/ analyte/ drug holding capacity, large porosity, biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-cytotoxicity, and stimuli responsivity (temperature and pH-sensitive) making it perfect candidate for drug delivery probes [2,3].

In the last few years, nanomaterials and their composite hydrogels have drawn the attention of researchers and biomedical applications due to their fascinating drug delivery performance. Since, the structure of nanocomposite hydrogels resembles with macromolecular components in the human body, it can be successfully applied for real applications with less toxicity [4]. Previously, the loading of nanomaterials into a hydrogel matrix with various types of drugs and their drug delivery systems (DDS) have been studied [5]. But so far now, the mechanistic role of nanomaterials and drug loading into the 3D matrix of hydrogel and its influence on the DDS is of great interest. The drug loading method and its interaction with functionalities on the hydrogel network can largely influence the controlled DDS depending on the rapid, burst, moderate, and slow release of the drug from the hydrogel matrix. Despite that, the hydrogels have some drawbacks like uncontrolled swelling which affects the release properties of drug [6].

The controlled DDS plays a vital role in the safer, targeted, and effective treatment with fewer side effects. The conventional DDS can be successfully replaced with biocompatible hydrogel-based systems for enhanced results with minimal drug doses. The hydrophilicity of hydrogels can lead to better loading of drugs as well as analytes into free spaces of vacant pores inside the hydrogel matrix. The stimuli responsiveness (pH, temperature, light, magnetic field, electric field), targeted, smart, and local necessity responsive controlled drug delivery vehicle can be achieved with the help of drug-loaded hydrogel probes [7,8].

The alginate-based hydrogel systems have been extensively studied as drug delivery carriers. As the sodium salt of alginic acid i.e., sodium alginate (S-Alg) is a biopolymer having biocompatibility, biodegradability and no immunogenic toxicity, and easily crosslink with divalent cationic metals (Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+) [9,10]. The -OH and -COOH functionalities present in S-Alg are easily available for electrostatic interaction with various analytes, drugs, and carbon dots, etc. [5,6]. The rapidly and easily gelling, hydrophilic, swelling nature as well as drug carrying capacity, and no interference with drug and ion exchange capacity makes it a perfect candidate for drug delivery vehicle [11]. Despite that, S-Alg having structural similarity and biocompatibility with extracellular matrices of living organisms [8].

However, from variety of carbonaceous precursors, carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent materials from the carbon fmily as well as easily and abundantly made available which are nontoxic, and extremely lower in size (<10 nm) [9]. The pioneer investigation of fluorescent CDs by Xiaoyou Xu and co-workers has opened a new research window and still today the progress and development of CDs based systems are of great interest [12]. The robust physicochemical properties of CDs viz. almost nontoxic, tunable optical properties, chemical inertness, water solubility, and high biocompatibility, etc. hence, these functional CDs are a promising candidate in DDS [9,13]. The fluorescent CDs are smaller particles that can be easily penetrated into the cavities of hydrogel matrix with physicochemical interactions (ionic, weak Van dar Walls, hydrogen bonding) [14]. In 2016, the research group of Devasish Chowdhury used CDs coated hydrogels as a model for tetracycline DDS [5]. In addition, Sarat K. Swain et al. have reported that Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) tailored S-Alg hydrogel film for targeted delivery of vancomycin antibiotic [9]. Till now, mostly the DDS were developed to study the fixed drug release and not according to stimuli-responsive as well as controlled and or burst release-based systems. To overcome the drawbacks of previous DDS, we have tried to develop the fluorescent WTR-CDs as a model drug loaded S-Alg hydrogel systems and its WTR-CDs loading method dependent release behaviour. From the literature survey, the various biopolymeric hydrogel system loaded with different analytes as a delivery probe and their respective applications are discussed in Table 1 .

The biocompatibility, and biological applicability of S-Alg hydrogel beads (HB-Alg) can be studied by using an in-vivo Chick Chorioallantoic Membrane (CAM) assay. In the process of angiogenesis, the new blood vessels formation takes places by endothelial cell migration and splitting of vessels [15]. Angiogenesis is the controlled development of blood vessels in the biological systems [16]. In contrast, the reduction and disorganization of microscopic blood vessels leading to the anti-angiogenesis activity, which can be used to evaluate the inhibition or prevention of tumour and cancer [17]. The fluorescent CDs have been previously reported as a potential anti-angiogenic applications by inhibiting the formation of blood vessels [15,18].

The waste tea residue biomass derived CDs (WTR-CDs) were already developed by our laboratory for sensing applications [19]. In present investigation, with further modification and next step development, the highly fluorescent WTR-CDs loaded HB-Alg with different loading pathways were modulated and loading method dependent release behaviour was studied. In addition, to check the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the developed WTR-CDs loaded S-Alg hydrogel systems, the in-vivo CAM assay was investigated.

The waste tea residue (WTR) powder was collected from the kitchen as a biowaste material after the preparation of tea; containing milk, sugar, and ginger as other ingredients (Kolhapur, India). The biopolymer sodium alginate (low viscosity; 4–12 cps, G:M = 0.99, purity = 99.0 %) and its crosslinker calcium chloride (extra pure, AR, M.W. 147.02, purity = 99.5 %) were procured from Sisco Research Laboratories, PVT. LTD. (SRL). The fertilized eggs were purchased from the Government of Maharashtra Egg Incubation Centre, Kolhapur. All other chemicals used throughout the experiment were of analytical grade. The complete experimental procedures were carried out using doubly distilled water (D.W.).

The optical properties of WTR-CDs were characterised by using a double beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (SPECORD 210 Plus, Analytikjena, Germany), JASCO FP-8300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). The surface functionalities of WTR-CDs and HB-Alg systems were studied by using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker). While, the X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) of WTR-CDs, HB-Alg, and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C beads were performed on (Bruker, D8 Advance), using CuKα radiations. The particle size and morphology of WTR-CDs was studied using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEOL, Voltage of 300 kV) images were obtained by using a copper grid. Also, the elemental composition and elemental mapping of WTR-CDs was studied by using EDX (JEM-ARM-200F, voltage of 200 kV, Oxford). The surface morphology of HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C was investigated by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM, SNE-4500 M Plus Tabletop SEM). The elemental analysis of WTR-CDs was performed with X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) over a binding energy range of 0 to 800 eV using MgKα (1253.6 eV) as an X-Ray source (JEOL, JPS 9030). The thermal stability and degradation of HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C was evaluated using Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (SDT 650) at heating rate of 10 °C/min in presence of air atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of waste tea residue carbon dots (WTR-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The kitchen waste valorisation approach was employed for the synthesis of highly luminescent waste tea residue biomass-derived carbon dots (WTR-CDs). The WTR-CDs were prepared with little modification by following a simple, cost-effective carbonization method previously reported by our research group [19]. The leftover residual waste tea from the kitchen containing sugar, milk, and ginger as additional ingredients were collected in bulk and dried at room temperature. The dried WTR was finely grounded and 5.0 g powder was collected in a silica crucible and employed for simple carbonization in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 5 h (Scheme 1 ). Then, the carbonized black WTR powder dispersed into D.W. and continuously stirred overnight, and allowed to settle down the residual content. The WTR-CDs were extracted and purified by filtration of supernatant solution through Whatman filter paper No. 41 and centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 20 min). Further, the purified WTR-CDs were passed through a syringe filter (0.22 μm) to eliminate larger particles. The homogeneous and uniform-sized aqueous solution of WTR-CDs was diluted to a specific volume and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The hydrogel beads of S-Alg (HB-Alg) was prepared by a simple, one-step method, in which S-Alg solution was drop casted into an aqueous solution of CaCl2 (ionotropic/extrusion dripping method) [5,27]. In this process, the 10 mL of freshly prepared 5 % (w/v) S-Alg solution, as well as the 10 mL of 5 % (w/v) CaCl2 solution was prepared (Fig. 1 ). The S-Alg solution was dropwise added using a 5 mL syringe into the solution containing Ca2+ ions under continuous stirring conditions. As the S-Alg solution comes in contact with Ca2+ ions, instantly the formation of spherical shaped calcium ions crosslinked alginate beads takes place by rapid gelation via chemical crosslinking. The calcium crosslinked alginate hydrogel beads (HB-Alg) were as it is placed into a CaCl2 solution up to 3 h for complete gelation of hydrogel beads. Then the resultant HB-Alg were washed several times with D.W. to remove the chloride ions. The HB-Alg were prepared into stock and dried in an oven at 65 °C for 12 h and stored in air pack container for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of WTR-CDs loaded alginate hydrogel beads (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A series of WTR-CDs loaded alginate hydrogel beads (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs) with different WTR-CDs loading methods were prepared as per the following method (Fig. 1). All the three different possible routes for loading of WTR-CDs were followed. The In-situ loading of WTR-CDs into hydrogel beads was prepared by 1 g of S-Alg added to 20 mL of WTR-CDs aqueous solution with continuous stirring until formation of homogeneous mixture. Then the WTR-CDs dispersed S-Alg solution was dropped into 20 mL of 5 % (w/v) CaCl2 solution and allowed to crosslink for 3 h and washed several times with D.W. to remove excess CaCl2 and surface adhered WTR-CDs and then dried in an oven at 65 °C for 12 h and noted as HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A. In next type of WTR-CDs loading method, the pristine HB-Alg beads were prepared according to abovementioned protocol. The HB-Alg in wet condition (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B) as well as in dry condition (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C) were added into 20 mL WTR-CDs solution separately. The beads were soaked for overnight into WTR-CDs solution for maximum uptake of WTR-CDs into hydrogel matrix. Then washed with D.W. to remove surface adhered WTR-CDs, dried in an oven and stored.

Swelling ratio is defined as the fractional increase in the weight of hydrogel due to water absorption [28]. The swelling ratio of beads was determined by immersing the accurately weighted (50 mg) dried beads in deionised water (D.W.) in triplicate sets (n = 3). The weight of swollen beads was recorded after time interval of 0, 30, 60, 90, 120 up to 360 min. The excess water on surface of swollen beads was gently wiped by tissue paper and weight of swollen beads was recorded. The swelling ratio was calculated by using Eq. (1).
(1)

W D

Where, WS = Weight of beads after swelling, WD = Weight of dry beads.

The fluorescence efficiency of WTR-CDs was quantified by measuring the quantum yield (QY). In present study, the standard comparison method was employed to calculate the QY. The well-known highly fluorescent quinine sulphate (QS) was used as a standard reference material. The aqueous solution of WTR-CDs and quinine sulphate (ϕQS = 0.54, refractive index η = 1.33) in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used and absorbance was kept below 0.10. The emission spectra of both sample and reference were recorded at same excitation wavelength at λex = 380 nm. The absorbance and fluorescence of respective solutions were recorded and the ϕ of WTR-CDs was calculated by using formula given below (Eq. (2)) [29,30].
(2)

Where, Φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, whereas A is the maximum absorbance as well as η is the refractive index of QS and WTR-CDs respectively.

The WTR-CDs loading/ entrapment efficiency was assessed by spectrofluorometric method at excitation wavelength of λex = 380 nm. The loading of WTR-CDs into HB-Alg beads via three different routes were calculated using Eq. (3) and the amount of WTR-CDs loaded into HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A,B,C hydrogel beads were calculated with the help of calibration curve. The calibration curve of different concentration of WTR-CDs was plotted with R2 = 0.9751 (Fig. S1). The known concentration of WTR-CDs was equally added to each HB-Alg beads using different methods and the percentage of WTR-CDs entrapment efficiency was evaluated (Table 2 ).

The carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanoscale materials having tuneable surface functionalization and luminescence properties. These tiny materials can be easily amalgamated/ entrapped into hydrogel matrix and makes interaction with hydrogel network via weak Van der Walls forces, ionic, hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions [31]. These distinguishable properties of CDs can be studied to understand the fundamentals of mechanistic stimuli responsive and controlled release from the hydrogel.

As per earlier discussion, the WTR-CDs were loaded into HB-Alg matrix via all three possible routes and further used to modulate the loading method dependent stimuli responsive and controlled release behaviour. The functionalized HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C loaded hydrogel beads were designed to release the WTR-CDs as a model probe for WTR-CDs delivery studied at three different pH environments. The pH responsive WTR-CDs release behaviour was investigated at different pH viz. (Acidic pH = 3.0, Neutral pH = 7.4, and Basic pH = 9.0). In the present experiment, the HB-Alg and WTR-CDs loaded beads were placed into a release medium of 10.0 mL of which 3.0 mL of the released aliquot was withdrawn periodically and recorded the fluorescence and again added back to maintain the volume of the release medium. The WTR-CDs release study was performed at different time intervals of 1, 3, 6, 12, 24, 36, 48 h and extended up to 7 successive days. The fluorescence of released aliquot from pristine HB-Alg was eliminated from all systems and the percentage cumulative release was calculated using standard calibrations curve. The WTR-CDs release was calculated by correlating the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm and the same experiment was conducted in triplicate (n = 3).

We have carried out a commonly used DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) radical scavenging study to evaluate antioxidant activity of waste tea residue bagasse (WTR-Powder), WTR-CDs, HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, and C. Simple UV–vis absorption studies were carried out to evaluate the antioxidant activity. The equally weighed samples were added into 2 mL of D.W. for 1 h and prepared the sample solutions and the accurately weighed DPPH was dissolved in methanol to prepare 0.02 mmol/L stock solution. Then, 100 μL of each abovementioned solution was separately added to the 100 μL of DPPH solution. All the above mentioned mixtures were incubated in dark condition for 30 min and the reaction progress of antioxidant activity was monitored by UV–Vis absorbance changes at 517 nm. The introduced samples reacts with deep violet coloured stable DPPH free radical solution and upon reaction the solution colour changes to light yellow with its conversion to DPPH-H complex [32,33] (Eq. (4)). The present study was carried out in triplicate to check the reproducibility of the results (n = 3).
(4)
Scavanging Activity
%
=
Absorbance of Control − Absorbance of Sample
Absorbance of Control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: The chicken chorioallantoic membrane (CAM) assay for angiogenesis study
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of angiogenesis for pristine HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C beads was performed on fertilized chicken eggs. The zero-day fertilized eggs were purchased from the egg incubation centre, then initially cleaned and sanitized in laminar air hood with 70 % ethanol to avoid the microbial infections. Afterwards, the eggs were placed in an incubator in vertical position having blunt end upside and pointed end towards downside with experimental conditions maintained at 37 °C and at relative humidity of 80 %. Then after 3 days of incubation period 1.0 mL of albumin was injected out using syringe to maintain the vacuum inside the eggs. Further, on the air sac side of the eggs, a window of 1–2 cm was opened and then the samples were introduced to the CAM. The opened cavity of egg shell was sealed using parafilm and eggs were incubated at abovementioned experimental conditions for 7 days. The obtained results were photographed to study the newly formed blood vessels in CAM (Fig. 11). The present experiment was performed in triplicate to minimize the technical problems and to check the consistency of the results (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of WTR-CDs, HB-Alg, and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C beads
ce:section.ce:para: The biowaste derived highly fluorescent blue light emitting WTR-CDs were confirmed via different spectroscopic methods. As well as chemical composition and interactions of WTR-CDs with HB-Alg were investigated using different analytical and instrumentation methods to understand the morphology as well as the loading and release behaviour of WTR-CDs from the hydrogel probe.

The optical properties of WTR-CDs were confirmed by using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy to explore the absorbance and emission nature of WTR-CDs. Two humps were noticed in the UV–Vis spectra at 260 nm and 330 nm corresponds to the π-π⁎ and n-π⁎ transitions due to the presence of CC and CO functionalities, which were further revealed by FTIR analysis. Also, the emission of WTR-CDs was confirmed through fluorescence spectroscopy showing the broad emission peak at 450 nm with 380 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 2a). Further, to understand more about fluorescence behaviour, the WTR-CDs were exposed with different excitation wavelengths and their corresponding fluorescence spectrums recorded (Fig. 2b). The prominent changes were observed exhibiting that, as we change the excitation wavelength there is change in the emission wavelength. It is noticed that, the change in fluorescence emission is closely interrelated with the change in excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 400 nm with a bathochromic shift. The change in emission wavelength with respect to the excitation wavelength proves the excitation dependent emission of WTR-CDs [29]. Furthermore, the quantification of fluorescence properties was evaluated by determining the QY in comparison with QS as standard and QY of WTR-CDs was found to be 18.45 %. The highly fluorescent WTR-CDs are having excellent optical properties and it can be used as a suitable fluorescent probe in drug delivery probes.

The obtained WTR-CDs were dispersed into D.W. and its emission colour was observed under 365 nm UV lamp. The photographic images of WTR-CDs under day light and UV light proving the homogeneous dispersion and hydrophilic nature (scheme 1). The bright blue coloured emission of WTR-CDs under UV light is due to the quantum entanglement and localized electronic transitions in the framework of carbon dots [34].

The particle size and morphological evaluation of WTR-CDs was keenly explored by TEM analysis. The TEM images showing the monodispersed quasi spherical shaped WTR-CDs (Fig. 3 a-b). Furthermore, the Fig. 3d shows the histogram of WTR-CDs having particle diameter in range of 2–6 nm with an average diameter of 3.60 nm. The microstructure of as synthesized WTR-CDs was studied by HR-TEM analysis, which reveals that the TEM images in inset having well resolved lattice fringes with spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) in plane lattice spacing of graphene (Fig. 3c) [35]. As well as the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern WTR-CDs clearly illustrates the homocentric faded circular rings confirming the amorphous nature of carbon dots (Fig. 3e) [36]. Also, the calculated lattice facet from SAED pattern showing the (002) planes which corresponds to the spacing between graphene layers [37]. Additionally, the (100) plane of SAED pattern well matches with the HR-TEM results.

The elemental composition in WTR-CDs is confirmed by EDX and elemental mapping analysis. The Fig. 3f confirms the prominent presence of carbon with 69.75 %, oxygen with 28.38 % and 1.87 % of nitrogen. Interestingly, the results in elemental mapping showing the uniform distribution of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in WTR-CDs core (Fig. 3g).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) findings were studied to investigate the surface functional groups as well as chemical structure of WTR-CDs. Herein, the Fig. 4a depicts the full scan XPS survey spectrum with the binding energy (BE) ranging from 200 eV to 800 eV having three pronounced peaks at 284 eV, 399 eV and 531 eV confirming the presence of C1s, N1s and O1s elements respectively. The results are well consistent with the EDX data of WTR-CDs (Fig. 3f). Furthermore, to determine the configuration of C, N and O best Gaussian fits of above elements and their high-resolution deconvolution spectra were analyzed. In high resolution spectra, the C1s can be deconvoluted in to four peaks corresponding to, sp3 carbons (CC/CH, 283.6 eV) sp2 carbons (CC, 284.37 eV), sp3 carbons (CN, 285.49 eV), and carbonyl carbons (C=O/ CN, 287.25 eV) (Fig. 4b) [38]. The N1s peak can be deconvoluted into four peaks attributed to the CN, NH, CN, and NO peaks corresponding to 397.91 eV, 398.77 eV, 399.51 eV and 400.63 eV binding energies respectively (Fig. 4c) [39,40]. Additionally, Fig. 4d shows, O1s contains two deconvoluted peaks corresponding to the CO (530.45 eV), and CO (532.01 eV) [41,42]. The XPS and FTIR data obtained are in good agreement and clearly indicates that the WTR-CDs consisted with π conjugated domains at the carbon core with abundant presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and other hydrophilic functional groups leading to good interaction with other functionalities.

The surface functionalities, and structural composition of WTR-CDs, pristine HB-Alg and WTR-CDs loaded hydrogel beads (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C) was investigated by FTIR analysis. The Fig. 5a demonstrates the WTR-CDs and hydrogel beads are abundantly rich in oxygenated and hydrophilic surface functional groups viz. carboxylic, carbonyl, and hydroxy groups [22,38,39]. In Fig. 5a, the stretching vibrations of various functionalities at bands viz. 3342 cm−1 (-O-H/-N-H), 1615 cm−1 (C=C/ CO), and bands at 1090 cm−1, and 1030 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching and bending vibrations of (CO) carboxylic and alcoholic functionalities. The presence of abovementioned bands on WTR-CDs surface makes it hydrophilic and helps to interact with functional groups present in polymer hydrogels.

The broadening of band in the range from 3600 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1 in alginate and WTR-CDs loaded hydrogel beads is due to the extensive hydrogen bonding between -O-H groups of carbon dots and functional groups present in sodium alginate [5]. The band at 1254 cm−1 belongs to the -OH bending vibrations. Also, the bands at 2923 cm−1, and 2857 cm−1 are due to the -C-H stretching vibrations of methyl (-CH3) and methylene (-CH2) groups. The bands at 1615 cm−1 and 1425 cm−1 belongs to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylic (-COO−) groups, which electrostatically binds with the functional groups of WTR-CDs. The bands at 1090 cm−1, and 1030 cm−1 are caused by strong signals of guluronate (C-O-C) stretching and mannuronate (-O-H) bending vibrations [43]. Also, additional bands below 800 cm−1 are characteristic bands of Ca2+ ions used as a crosslinking agent.

The phase, purity, crystallinity, and structural properties of the samples were examined by X-ray diffraction analysis. Fig. 5b displays the XRD patterns of pristine WTR-CDs, HB-Alg as well as WTR-CDs loaded hydrogel beads (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C). The XRD pattern of WTR-CDs is having broad peak cantered at 23.6° (d = 0.23 nm) with (002) plane showing highly disordered and amorphous nature [44]. The pristine HB-Alg is typically having semi crystalline nature due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding into polymer matrix [45]. Three characteristic diffraction peaks present at different 2θ positions viz. broad peak at around 11.85° (110), 21.74° (200), and 41.44° (311) corresponds to the calcium alginate [45,46]. The abovementioned major diffraction peaks of polyguluronate units of alginate at (110) plane belongs to the α-1-guluronic acid as well as peak at (200) plane refers to the β-D-mannuronic acid [47]. Subsequently, all the three peaks present in HB-Alg retains same after successful loading of WTR-CDs into hydrogel matrix. In addition, there was no distinguishable change in peak position of alginate after incorporation of WTR-CDs into hydrogel matrix, which confirms the structural properties of hydrogel beads remains intact even after loading of WTR-CDs into hydrogel framework.

The surface morphology and elemental composition of HB-Alg and WTR-CDs loaded hydrogel beads (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs- A, B, C) was studied using FE-SEM and EDX analysis. Fig. 6a-d shows the nearly spherical shaped morphology of hydrogel beads. The wrinkled surface of beads may be caused by shrinking of beads due drying and partial collapse of alginate network [48,49]. At higher magnification, the topography of beads shows detectable pores which permits the analytes to entrap into egg box shaped polymer network. The crosslinked hydrogel network with highly porous nature was observed. In, Fig. 6b and 6c there is detectable change was observed due to crosslinking of WTR-CDs into matrix of crosslinked beads than pristine HB-Alg beads. After loading of WTR-CDs, the beads showed considerable change in morphology and surface covered with granules. The Fig. 6d showing significant change in surface morphology of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C beads. The shrinking of pores was due to drying of beads and the WTR-CDs was found to be successful entrapped into pores of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C system.

As well as, the mapping of elements shows the prominently presence of C, O, N, Ca, Na, and Cl in hydrogel systems. Fig. 7 demonstrates the elemental composition of hydrogel beads with elemental mapping. There is majority presence of C, O, Na elements, which belong to the sodium alginate polymer. Also, Ca and Cl peaks are observed from CaCl2 as a crosslinking agent. On comparison, the nitrogen peaks were found in Fig. 7 b and c, indicating the successful decoration of WTR-CDs into polymer network beads as WTR-CDs containing nitrogen. In addition, the elemental mapping images of respective elements were shown in Fig. 7.

The results from FE-SEM, EDX and elemental mapping strongly in agreement that, there is prominent and distinguishable changes are observed in morphology and elemental composition of hydrogel beads. The different loading methods of WTR-CDs into hydrogel systems leads to confirm the presence of WTR-CDs with method dependent change into morphology and composition.

The swelling behaviour of the hydrogel is critically important parameter to understand structural stability, water retaining, releasing properties, and practical applicability. The swelling study of as prepared pristine and WTR-CDs loaded hydrogel beads were checked in D.W. as a swelling medium. The swelling ratio of all the systems were checked over a time period from 0 to 360 min.

The Fig. 8 showing the swelling behaviour of HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C beads. The swelling efficiency gradually increases from 22 % to 40 % within first hour. Subsequently, the controlled increase in swelling efficiency was found to be in the range of 40 % to 60 % within 240 min. After that, saturation in swelling of hydrogel beads was noticed. The swelling efficiency of HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C was found to be 62.3 ± 1.87 %, 65.2 ± 2.60 %, 68.8 ± 2.40 %, and 61.4 ± 1.86 % respectively within 360 min respectively (Table 2). In comparison, HB-Alg@WTR-CDs- A and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B having improved swelling efficiency than pristine HB-Alg and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs- C showing the porous nature of hydrogel beads.

The thermal stability and degradation of the material is one of the important properties analyzed by thermogravimetric techniques (TGA). In present study, the TGA of pristine HB-Alg and WTR-CDs loaded HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C was performed to measure the weight loss up to 500 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min under air atmosphere (Fig. S2). Primarily, in all systems very small weight loss of around 8 % which was starting from 50 °C up to 100 °C was due to evaporation of volatile materials, and moisture [6]. Further, the weight loss of 15 % up to 186 °C was due to dehydration of absorbed water and loss of polymer bound structural water. The next weight loss was observed between 200 and 250 °C with a loss of 31 % indicating the breakdown of complexed polymer network as well as there may be degradation of WTR-CDs takes place. Further, from 250 to 300 °C region, the decomposition of polymer network and partial oxidation of alginate takes place [9]. In the final stage of degradation onwards 350 °C the complete degradation of biogenic materials takes place and at last remains residual content. The residual content of HB-Alg and WTR-CDs loaded HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C remains 40.33 %, 43.47 %, 38.06 % and 33.07 % respectively. The slight change in residual content suggesting that incorporation of WTR-CDs does not impart any structural deformities and retains their structural stability. The WTR-CDs loaded alginate hydrogel showing good thermal stability due to egg box structure of alginate network [50].

The comparative WTR-CDs loading method dependent pH responsive WTR-CDs release profile of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C was monitored at different pH environments viz. 3.0 (acidic), 7.4 (neutral), and 9.0 (basic) (Fig. 9 a-c). Also, the fluorescence of released aliquot from pristine HB-Alg was monitored and showing very small fluorescence which was eliminated from all the systems. The systematic WTR-CDs release profile was monitored using fluorescence spectrophotometer over period of time. To obtain the WTR-CDs release efficiency of different systems, the same study was carried out for three times to validate the reproducibility of the developed probes and results were calculated using calibration plot of WTR-CDs (Fig. S1). The results obtained were showing promising data and indicating that the WTR-CDs released in acidic, neutral and basic medium are having method dependent release behaviour instead of pH effect. The comparative assessment of results proving that, within first 3 h, the HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B system shows 38.59 ± 2.01 % to 54.98 ± 2.92 % of WTR-CDs release at every pH monitored. This seems to be having burst release of WTR-CDs from the hydrogel matrix. Though, the HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A system displaying the gradual WTR-CDs release with 2.31 ± 0.52 % to 16.25 ± 1.55 % of cumulative release. Whereas, in case of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C systems the release profile was below 5 %. The rate of WTR-CDs release is consistent in HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B system with 52.08 ± 2.70 to 58.59 ± 4.56 % cumulative release after 6 to 48 h, whereas, for HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A system percentage increased up to 35.53 ± 1.96 % of the loaded WTR-CDs were released. Interestingly, after 3 days to 7 days the release of WTR-CDs was increased and further saturated from 59.22 ± 3.93 % to 65.61 ± 5.79 % and 36.10 ± 1.88 % to 41.40 ± 3.50 % in case of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B, and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A respectively. Whereas, in case of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C the cumulative % release of WTR-CDs was remains below 12.28 ± 2.58 % up to 7 days. The WTR-CDs release efficiency of all the hydrogel systems are summarized in Table 2.

The WTR-CDs incorporated in wet HB-Alg (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B) weakly binds to the polymer network with weak Van der Walls, and π interactions, which in turn releases rapidly. Despite that, the in-situ loaded WTR-CDs into polymer blends (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A) makes hydrogen bonding and ionic interactions with functional groups of alginates. The crosslinked WTR-CDs into hydrogel retains into matrix for long and sustain release is obtained. The cumulative release graph of HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C is having relatively low release of WTR-CDs this indicates that, there is low uptake of WTR-CDs due to shrinkage of surface in HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C system. The obtained results are in good agreement and confirming that there was slight effect of pH on the release behaviour of WTR-CDs rather, the WTR-CDs loading method is promisingly influencing the stimuli responsive and controlled release.

The interaction of WTR-CDs with hydrogel matrix can be mechanistically represented as Scheme 2 . The results and characterizations obtained for WTR-CDs and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C hydrogel put forth the next step for understanding the different methods of loading WTR-CDs emphasizing the prominent role in the interaction with functional groups of S-Alg. From the results, it confirms that, the WTR-CDs added in-situ while preparation of beads making ionic interactions with S-Alg in HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A methods. While, in other case, the WTR-CDs loaded in wet gel gets easily penetrated into hydrogel matrix and making hydrogen bonding and weak interactions and in contrast, the WTR-CDs loaded in dry beads cannot penetrate easily into hydrogel beads due to shrinking of pores and loosely bound with weak Van der Waals forces with hydrogel network. The results suggest that, the WTR-CDs from HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A system shows controlled release, while the HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B system is having burst release and in HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C, the loosely bound WTR-CDs showing relatively low loading of WTR-CDs and cumulative release.

The antioxidant activity of Vit-C as standard as well as WTR-Powder, WTR-CDs, HB-Alg, and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C beads was explored by exposing the materials with DPPH radicals (Fig. 10 ). The Vit-C was used as a control for radical scavenging activity. In present study, the free radicals scavenging activity of Vit-C was highest as it was used as a control with 91.85 ± 2.75 %. Also, the free radical scavenging activity of synthesized WTR-CDs having promising activity in comparison with pristine WTR-Powder with antioxidant activity of 70.28 ± 3.50 % and 60.99 ± 0.86 % respectively. The antioxidant potency of WTR-CDs is higher as compared to WTR-Powder due to synthesized WTR-CDs having small size with rich in surface functionalities. In addition, the HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A is having 58.17 ± 1.56 % and 53.84 ± 3.11 % radial scavenging efficiency respectively. In contrast, the pristine HB-Alg (31.62 ± 3.04 %), and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C (46.47 ± 2.44 %) are having comparatively less radical scavenging efficiency. The results proving that the WTR-CDs synthesized from WTR-Powder is potent antioxidant candidate for free radical scavenging activity. Also, the rapid release from HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B system and controlled release of CDs from HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A system are showing distinguishable results. Whereas, the pristine HB-Alg beads and HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C systems are having comparatively less activity.

The CAM assay is prominently considered for angiogenesis study showing the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of the materials [16]. The ease of operation and cost effectiveness are the advantages of present study, in addition to that, the formed vascular network in CAM assay of fertilized chick embryo can be directly observed.

The CAM assay was employed in triplicate to explore the repeatability and angiogenic potential of samples viz. control, WTR-CDs, pristine HB-Alg and WTR-CDs loaded HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C. The growth in vascular network of CAM assays for above samples were photographed at 4th, 5th, and 7th days and angiogenesis study was monitored. The results obtained are summarized in Fig. 11 showing the promising angiogenic results. In all samples, the blood vessels are significantly developed and no loss of chick embryo was reported. In connection to this, from 4th day to 7th day there was increased number of blood vessels signifies that the employed samples having good biocompatibility. The waste tea residue carbon dots (WTR-CDs) and their composite hydrogels (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A, B, C) shown to be having comparatively no toxicity on CAM assay, which in turn can be used in various potential biomedical applications.

In present study, the sustainable and waste valorisation approach was employed for the synthesis of highly fluorescent WTR-CDs. The WTR-CDs were confirmed for their physico-chemical and optical properties using various characterization techniques. The WTR-CDs having average particle size in range of 2–6 nm enriched with C, N and O functionalities available for interaction with polymer network. The biopolymeric sodium alginate-based hydrogel beads (HB-Alg) were prepared and optimized by crosslinking with CaCl2 through extrusion method. The various routes for incorporation of WTR-CDs into polymeric network were exhibited viz. HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-A in which WTR-CDs were added while preparation of beads, in next case the WTR-CDs loaded into beads in wet condition (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-B) whereas, in last method the WTR-CDs loaded into dry hydrogel beads (HB-Alg@WTR-CDs-C). In connection to this, the abovementioned beads were employed to monitor the WTR-CDs release behaviour in different pH (pH = 3.0, 7.4, 9.0) environments. The results indicated that, the WTR-CDs loaded in dry beads shows less release, while, the WTR-CDs loaded in wet beads shows burst release, whereas, the in-situ WTR-CDs loaded method shows sustained release. These stimuli responsive release behaviour of WTR-CDs have shown interesting results indicating that the release behaviour is greatly influenced by method of loading. The need-based (sustained, controlled or burst) release behaviour can be achieved by abovementioned protocols. The radical scavenging activity of WTR-Powder, WTR-CDs and different hydrogel systems was studied and shown good antioxidant activity. In addition, the biocompatibility of the pristine and WTR-CDs loaded HB-Alg@WTR-CDs beads were applied for CAM assay and there was significant formation of blood vascular network and development of embryo indicated that, the developed beads are biocompatible. All the applied materials on CAM assay showed promising angiogenic activity on 7th day. Herein, the strategy presented for modulation of stimuli responsive as well as biocompatible delivery system which has effectively influenced by method of WTR-CDs loading. The developed protocol successfully employed for monitoring WTR-CDs release behaviour to develop smart and method dependent controlled release probe. The present work greatly focuses on kitchen waste derived synthesis of highly fluorescent WTR-CDs and their sodium alginate based biopolymeric hydrogel probes for cumulative release and biocompatibility study. This study will broadly help to develop low cost, smart, sustainable and biocompatible materials with highly efficient stimuli responsive delivery systems for various biomedical applications.
